content.text: Based on the official resources from Multi-Agent Wiki, AWS documentation, and industry best practices,
a single event triggers multiple agents to execute concurrently in parallel
, dramatically improving speed and scalability compared to sequential execution.

attrs.alt: Event fan-out for parallel agent activation concept diagram

content.text: architectural pattern whereby a single source sends messages to multiple destinations simultaneously
, commonly implemented in pub/sub systems and parallel task queues.

Single Event
    ↓
Fan-out (distribute to multiple targets)
    ↓
Agent 1 → runs concurrently
Agent 2 → runs concurrently
Agent 3 → runs concurrently
    ↓
Fan-in (gather + synthesize results)
    ↓
Final output
splits a task across many agents in parallel; an aggregator merges the results.
Split task across multiple agents that execute concurrently
Branches share read-only context by default, never the workspace
Aggregator merges, votes on, or synthesizes results
: Multi-perspective exploration, parallel retrieval, parallel code reading, log analysis, model ensembling

Single event occurs (user action, incoming message, scheduled job)
    ↓
Initiates distribution process

System determines targets
    ↓
Prepares message/workload for multiple recipients
For data processing: workload may be split into smaller independent tasks

Messages/workloads distributed to multiple consumers in parallel
    ↓
Often by message brokers or application logic

Each consumer processes assigned task concurrently in isolation
    ↓
Specialized agents each handle distinct tasks parallel

: Agents scale independently based on their own queue depth
-

: Distributed processing reduces single points of failure
-
: Real-time adjustments based on streaming data

Process ends after all parallel tasks complete independently
    ↓
If one branch fails: retried/handled independently while others continue

Controller (Lambda/Step Function)
    ↓
Distributes subtasks using map state for parallel execution
    ↓
Each task invokes Bedrock LLM or subagent with its own prompt
    ↓
Results stored in S3/DynamoDB/queues
    ↓
Aggregation step merges, compares, or filters outputs

Event triggers Lambda
    ↓
Lambda invokes multiple functions with concurrency
    ↓
Each function runs agent task independently
    ↓
Coordinator merges results from multiple invoked functions
: "If 500 research requests come in at once, you get 500 research agent functions running in parallel. The outline agents don't even notice. They're completely independent".

Event先知 publishes to EventBridge topic
    ↓
Multiple subscribers (agents) receive event
    ↓
Use correlation IDs for tracking
    ↓
Aggregation workflows merge results

Controller manages fan-out and gather
    ↓
Uses S3, DynamoDB, or queues for coordination
    ↓
Each agent gets run id / trace id
Before fanning out, give each branch a distinct goal — avoid duplicated work
Branches share read-only context by default, never the workspace
Gather step does deduplication, conflict detection, evidence merging — not naive concatenation
Configure concurrency, timeouts, cancellation, and budget caps
Every agent call carries a run id / trace id
Failure, timeout, cancel, and retry strategies defined
Context passed is minimum required, not full history
High-risk actions are gated by approval or verifier
Track fan-out execution with these events:

fanout.started
branch.started
branch.completed
branch.timeout
gather.started
gather.completed
Multiple agents work simultaneously instead of sequentially
Slow agents don't block fast agents

Event occurs → System immediately pushes to all targets
    ↓
Pre-distributes event upfront
: Need immediate delivery to all subscribers

Event occurs → System stores in single source feed
    ↓
Consumer requests event → System then distributes
    ↓
Withholds distribution until consumer requests
: Subscribers have different rates, want on-demand fetching
Multiple agents research different aspects of query
Multiple agents review content from different angles
Multiple agents analyze different log categories
Multiple models produce outputs, aggregate votes
Multiple agents read different code sections
Event → Multiple agents (parallel) → Each completes independently

Agent 1 (upstream) → Output
    ↓
Fan-out to Agents 2, 3, 4 (concurrent)
    ↓
Fan-in to Agent 5 (synthesize)
: Previous execution model activated one agent at a time; fan-out-fan-in treats agents as concurrent participants for that step, running them parallel before converging results downstream.
a restaurant kitchen receiving a large order

: instead of one chef cooking everything sequentially (slow), the order (event) is distributed to 5 chefs simultaneously (fan-out), each chef works on their dish independently (parallel processing), and then the dishes are plated together by the expeditor (fan-in/gather). If one chef is slow or has an issue, the other dishes still get ready.
Fan-out = single event triggers multiple agents to execute concurrently in parallel
One input generates multiple outputs concurrently
Pattern: Fan-out (split task) → Execute (parallel) → Fan-in (gather + synthesize)
Use when: strongly parallelizable tasks, multi-perspective exploration, high throughput, reduce latency
Don't use when: strongly order-dependent, shared mutable state needed, tight budget
Implement via: AWS Step Functions (map state), Lambda concurrency, EventBridge, custom controller
Before fanning out: give each branch distinct goal to avoid duplicated work
Branches share read-only context by default, never workspace
Gather does: deduplication, conflict detection, evidence merging — NOT naive concatenation
Fan-out-on-write (push): immediate delivery to all targets; Fan-out-on-read (pull): on-demand fetching
Track with events: fanout.started, branch.started/completed/timeout, gather.started/completed
Configuration: concurrency, timeouts, cancellation, budget caps

Agents scale the same way Lambda always has — if 500 research requests come in at once, you get 500 research agent functions running in parallel, and the outline agents don't even notice because they're completely independent.
When working with event fan-out for parallel agent activation, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with event fan-out for parallel agent activation. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of event fan-out for parallel agent activation scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
